config.correctionTitle: Correction from the advanced training material

trainingEvaluation.title: Training evaluation
trainingEvaluation.intro: Please provide a concise evaluation of the training. Your feedback is submitted privately with your assessment result and is used to improve future sessions.
trainingEvaluation.scaleLabel: Rating scale: 1 = insufficient, 2 = needs improvement, 3 = satisfactory, 4 = strong, 5 = excellent.

criteria.label: Relevance to consulting work and day-to-day delivery
Clarity of LLM fundamentals, agent concepts and tool-routing principles
Practical applicability of examples, workflows and exercises
Confidence in applying governance, permissions and human-review gates
Confidence in knowing when and how to switch to Codex-style coding agents
Quality, structure and professionalism of the training materials
Balance between pace, depth and time for questions
Overall satisfaction with the training session

questions.q: A team asks an LLM to build a source-backed market-entry recommendation from public articles, public investor decks, and a few uploaded documents already authorized as Green or otherwise permitted for AI use. Which setup best follows the advanced routing discipline?
questions.source: Advanced model routing and AI-use charter compatibility - combine a reasoning model with only the necessary approved tools, use only public or Green/permitted documents, define the source perimeter and output schema, sample-check first, and keep human review.

options.text: Use instant mode without tools, because the inputs are public or authorized and the final answer can be checked later.
options.why: Incorrect. Public or authorized inputs do not remove the need for source-aware tools, deliberate reasoning, and controlled evidence review.

options.text: Enable every connector and let the model decide which sources are relevant.
options.why: Incorrect. The training emphasizes least-necessary tooling and an explicit source perimeter, not maximum autonomy.

options.text: Use image generation to draft the market-entry story, then infer the evidence from the visual.
options.why: Incorrect. Image generation is not an evidence workflow and cannot replace source-backed analysis.

options.text: Use a reasoning model with required tools, an authorized source perimeter, a fixed schema, sample checks, and human review.
options.why: Correct. This combines model routing, source discipline, charter-compatible document authorization, schema discipline, and the human review gate.

options.text: Use GPT Pro for every step and skip the source perimeter because the model is more capable.
options.why: Incorrect. Higher-capability models do not remove source boundaries, tool boundaries, sample checks, or review.

questions.q: An LLM produces a confident market-size calculation with a neat table but no traceable sources or formula audit. What is the right status of that output?
questions.source: LLM fundamentals - LLM output is a draft hypothesis, not proof; sources, logic, numbers, and confidentiality must be checked before use.

options.text: A draft hypothesis requiring checks of evidence, logic, calculations, and confidentiality.
options.why: Correct. The training explicitly warns that fluent and confident output is not proof.

options.text: A validated answer because the table is structured and the model sounds confident.
options.why: Incorrect. Formatting and confidence do not validate facts, formulas, assumptions, or sources.

options.text: A final internal answer because only external deliverables require verification.
options.why: Incorrect. Internal work product can still create downstream risk and must be reviewed.

options.text: A source-backed analysis because market sizing is a standard LLM strength.
options.why: Incorrect. LLMs can assist with structure and drafts, but the answer is not source-backed unless evidence is actually checked.

options.text: A safe answer if the prompt asked the model not to hallucinate.
options.why: Incorrect. Prompt wording does not guarantee factuality or numerical correctness.

questions.q: A practice wants a weekly public deal-radar assistant that scans defined public sources, outputs a structured shortlist, and is reviewed by a named owner before sharing. Which deployment surface best fits?
questions.source: No-code agents - recurring source-backed work can be packaged as a saved assistant with role, knowledge, method, output checks, owner, tests, and sharing controls.

options.text: A one-off chat, because any saved workflow would be over-governed.
options.why: Incorrect. Recurrence, defined sources, and repeated output make this a strong candidate for a governed saved agent.

options.text: A fully autonomous agent that emails the shortlist directly to clients every week.
options.why: Incorrect. The training keeps review gates before sharing and does not support autonomous business actions without approval.

options.text: A coding agent by default, because every recurring workflow requires file edits and commands.
options.why: Incorrect. Coding agents fit file edits, commands, scale, and evidence generation; a source-backed recurring assistant can be no-code if bounded and reviewable.

options.text: A personal browser extension chosen by the participant, because the data is public.
options.why: Incorrect. Tool approval and governance still matter even when the source material is public.

options.text: A governed no-code shared agent with approved sources, tests, an owner, monitoring, and revocation.
options.why: Correct. This matches the no-code agent and shared deployment controls described in the training.

questions.q: A coding-agent workflow must extract fields for 80 companies. The pilot works on two companies, but the output schema is still changing. What should happen before parallel runs?
questions.source: Industrialized coding agents - scale only after scope, schema, tools, stop rules, logs, and sample QA are fixed.

options.text: Start parallel runs immediately so the final schema emerges from the full batch.
options.why: Incorrect. Parallelization before schema stability multiplies rework and error propagation.

options.text: Freeze scope, schema, tools, stop rules, logs, and QA criteria before scaling.
options.why: Correct. The advanced scaling rule is to lock the operating system before multiplying the run.

options.text: Delete pilot logs so the batch starts from a clean state.
options.why: Incorrect. Logs are evidence and should be retained for review and troubleshooting.

options.text: Move directly to client delivery if the first two examples look polished.
options.why: Incorrect. Polished output is not enough; the reviewer needs evidence, checks, and residual-risk review.

options.text: Switch to instant mode because repeated extraction is mechanical.
options.why: Incorrect. Repetition increases the need for schema discipline and checks; it does not justify reducing control.

questions.q: A proposed MCP connector would let an agent read CRM records that are authorized for this workflow, write notes, and open follow-up tasks. What is the most complete governance answer before use?
questions.source: MCP and connector governance - connectors expose allowed data, tools, or actions while access remains governed by authentication, permissions, logs, review, monitoring, revocation, and any AI-use charter restrictions on the underlying records.

options.text: Approve it if the agent prompt says not to change important records.
options.why: Incorrect. Prompt wording is not a permission, logging, or revocation control.

options.text: Approve it because MCP connectors are designed to bypass manual authentication.
options.why: Incorrect. The training states that authentication and permissions remain central.

options.text: Approve scoped data and actions with authentication, authorization, least privilege, logs, review, monitoring, and revocation.
options.why: Correct. This captures the full connector-control model and the required compatibility check for the underlying data.

options.text: Allow read and write access to all CRM objects so the agent can discover the right workflow.
options.why: Incorrect. Broad access violates least privilege and scope discipline.

options.text: Use the connector only in a personal run, because personal runs do not need deployment controls.
options.why: Incorrect. Personal runs still require appropriate permissions and review; sharing adds more controls but does not eliminate baseline governance.

questions.q: A coding agent needs one authorized workbook stored in a Documents folder that also contains unrelated client files. What is the least-privilege setup?
questions.source: Permission gates and least privilege - isolate or name only the authorized files, define allowed actions, and identify the reviewer instead of exposing a broad mixed folder.

options.text: Approve it because broad search is often needed at the start of a coding-agent run.
options.why: Incorrect. Initial exploration still needs a bounded workspace or explicit approval.

options.text: Approve it if the command is read-only, because read-only commands cannot create risk.
options.why: Incorrect. Read-only access can still expose unrelated or sensitive information and must respect scope.

options.text: Approve it if the agent promises not to edit unrelated files.
options.why: Incorrect. The scope boundary must be clear before the command, not inferred from the agent's promise.

options.text: Run it first and inspect logs afterward.
options.why: Incorrect. Logs are not a substitute for permission and boundary clarity.

options.text: Create a dedicated workspace with only authorized files, then define allowed actions and reviewer.
options.why: Correct. Isolating the authorized workbook prevents access to unrelated client files and makes the action and review boundaries explicit.

questions.q: A participant mixes an internal PDF corpus that is approved and Green/permitted for AI use with current public web news in one analysis. Which tool distinction must be preserved?
questions.source: Agent tools and AI-use charter compatibility - file_search retrieves from approved Green/permitted files or corpora; web_search provides current public web information and citations.

options.text: Use file_search for both, because web_search and file_search are interchangeable retrieval tools.
options.why: Incorrect. The training gives them different access routes and evidence implications.

options.text: Use file_search for authorized internal files and web_search for current public sources; review both source trails.
options.why: Correct. This preserves the routing distinction, the document authorization requirement, and the evidence-review requirement.

options.text: Use web_search for those Green/permitted internal PDFs because it is the only tool that provides citations.
options.why: Incorrect. Internal files that are approved and Green/permitted for AI use should be retrieved through file search or approved corpus tooling.

options.text: Avoid both tools and ask the base model to remember the documents and news.
options.why: Incorrect. The base model cannot be treated as a controlled source retrieval system.

options.text: Use image generation to summarize charts from the PDFs before checking sources.
options.why: Incorrect. Image generation is not the route for source retrieval or evidence validation.

questions.q: Which advanced task most clearly belongs to code_interpreter rather than web_search or a no-code saved assistant, assuming the files used are authorized as Green or otherwise permitted for AI use?
questions.source: Agent tools and AI-use charter compatibility - code_interpreter is sandboxed Python for authorized data, charts, file processing, reconciliation, and checks.

options.text: Finding the latest public article on a sector trend with citations.
options.why: Incorrect. Current public information with citations belongs to web_search.

options.text: Packaging a recurring qualitative memo workflow with fixed public sources and review rules.
options.why: Incorrect. That is a no-code agent candidate if bounded, recurring, and reviewable.

options.text: Drafting a short email that does not require files, calculations, or source retrieval.
options.why: Incorrect. A simple low-risk language task can usually stay in chat.

options.text: Reconciling authorized Excel extracts, detecting duplicates, charting results, and checking formulas.
options.why: Correct. This is authorized data/file processing and validation, which maps to code_interpreter.

options.text: Creating a conceptual slide background from a prompt.
options.why: Incorrect. Visual generation is an image-generation task, not code_interpreter by default.

questions.q: In the phrase 'no evidence, no scale', what counts as evidence in the coding-agent workflow?
questions.source: Review gate and conclusion - decisions come from artifacts, logs, checks, screenshots, metrics, QA notes, and unresolved risks, not confidence.

options.text: Artifacts, logs, checks, metrics, QA notes, and residual risks.
options.why: Correct. These are the concrete artifacts a reviewer can inspect before scaling.

options.text: The agent's confidence, plus a concise verbal summary.
options.why: Incorrect. Confidence is specifically not the basis for scaling decisions.

options.text: A clean-looking final deliverable, even if intermediate logs are unavailable.
options.why: Incorrect. A polished output alone does not prove scope, source, or execution quality.

options.text: The absence of user complaints after the first run.
options.why: Incorrect. Lack of complaints is not a pre-scale review artifact.

options.text: A model-generated statement that all instructions were followed.
options.why: Incorrect. The reviewer needs inspectable evidence, not self-certification.

questions.q: Monitoring shows that a shared agent started answering excluded client-data questions after its knowledge base was updated. What should the owner do?
questions.source: Shared deployment incident response - monitoring and revocation must let the owner stop access, preserve evidence, restore the approved source perimeter, retest, and authorize any return to service.

options.text: Leave the agent available while users report any other problematic answers.
options.why: Incorrect. Continued access can expose more excluded material; the owner should contain the issue first.

options.text: Delete the monitoring logs and rebuild the agent from memory.
options.why: Incorrect. Logs are evidence needed to understand the incident and validate the correction.

options.text: Pause access, preserve logs, restore the approved perimeter, retest, and obtain owner approval.
options.why: Correct. This uses monitoring and revocation to contain the issue, retain evidence, correct the source boundary, and require controlled re-approval.

options.text: Remove the excluded answers manually but keep the broadened knowledge base connected.
options.why: Incorrect. Editing outputs does not fix the unauthorized retrieval perimeter that caused the incident.

options.text: Add a prompt telling the agent to ignore client data and resume access immediately.
options.why: Incorrect. Prompt wording alone is not a reliable access control and does not replace containment, investigation, and retesting.

questions.q: An agent brief says: 'Review these authorized sources, identify acquisition targets, enrich with evidence, and draft a memo.' Which missing element most directly prevents a controlled run?
questions.source: Brief discipline - define objective, authorized inputs, boundaries, output schema, evidence, stop rules, and owner/reviewer before execution.

options.text: The prompt does not ask the agent to be creative.
options.why: Incorrect. Creativity is not the missing control for this governed workflow.

options.text: Explicit source boundaries, schema, evidence requirements, stop rules, and a reviewer.
options.why: Correct. Even with authorized sources, without these controls the run cannot be reviewed or scaled safely.

options.text: The brief mentions both evidence and memo drafting, which should never be combined.
options.why: Incorrect. Multi-step workflows can be split and governed; the issue is missing controls.

options.text: The brief should avoid any output schema because target screening requires judgment.
options.why: Incorrect. Judgment-heavy work benefits from schema and evidence discipline.

options.text: The brief should let the agent choose the reviewer after seeing the output.
options.why: Incorrect. The reviewer should be identified before execution.

questions.q: For a coding-agent run, what does the 'permission' step in the five-decision flow mainly decide?
questions.source: Five-decision flow - switch, permission, brief, review, deploy; permission controls scope, authorized files, commands, external access, and reviewer acceptance.

options.text: Whether the agent's answer is strategically correct.
options.why: Incorrect. Strategic correctness is assessed at review, not granted at permission.

options.text: Whether the slides are visually attractive.
options.why: Incorrect. Visual quality is not the core permission decision.

options.text: Whether the user personally likes the proposed workflow.
options.why: Incorrect. Preference is not the same as scope, access, and command authorization.

options.text: Whether the task should become a no-code agent instead.
options.why: Incorrect. Surface choice is the switch decision; permission is about allowed access and action.

options.text: Authorized files, commands, external access, and reviewer responsibility.
options.why: Correct. This is the operational control point before the agent acts, and it must respect document authorization.

questions.q: Which output schema is strongest for repeated target-company profiling by a coding agent?
questions.source: Long and repeated runs - clarify prompt, sources, output schema, sample-check first, and retain evidence.

options.text: A free-form paragraph for each company, because flexibility improves quality.
options.why: Incorrect. Free-form output is harder to validate, compare, and scale.

options.text: A slide-ready story only, with no source fields because citations can be added later.
options.why: Incorrect. Evidence should be captured during the run, not retrofitted.

options.text: A fixed schema with required fields, source URLs, missing-data flags, and no invented values.
options.why: Correct. This creates comparable, reviewable, and auditable output for repeated work.

options.text: A model-generated confidence score only, because confidence summarizes quality.
options.why: Incorrect. Confidence without evidence fields and missing-data handling is not enough.

options.text: A screenshot of the agent's final answer.
options.why: Incorrect. A screenshot may be evidence of output, but it is not a structured extraction schema.

questions.q: When is GPT Pro or the highest-capability model the right default choice under the training logic?
questions.source: Model routing - GPT Pro is positioned for the hardest or longest high-value analyses, not every standard task.

options.text: Hard, high-value analysis where added depth justifies cost and latency, with review.
options.why: Correct. This is the disciplined routing logic for the most capable model.

options.text: For every consulting task, because higher capability removes routing decisions.
options.why: Incorrect. The training explicitly routes by task type rather than always using the highest model.

options.text: For simple wording edits, because short tasks benefit most from maximum reasoning.
options.why: Incorrect. Simple low-risk language tasks can often use faster, lighter modes.

options.text: Whenever the output includes citations, because GPT Pro validates sources automatically.
options.why: Incorrect. Source validation remains a human and tooling responsibility.

options.text: Whenever the task uses authorized files, because file access alone determines model selection.
options.why: Incorrect. Authorized files influence tool setup, but model choice still depends on complexity and value.

questions.q: Two public sources conflict on a market metric. What should a governed agent or LLM workflow do?
questions.source: Evidence discipline - when sources matter, verify sources; when evidence conflicts, surface the conflict rather than fabricate certainty.

options.text: Average the two numbers silently so the deliverable has one clean answer.
options.why: Incorrect. Silent averaging hides source conflict and may create false precision.

options.text: Choose the number that best supports the emerging storyline.
options.why: Incorrect. Evidence should constrain the storyline, not be selected to fit it.

options.text: Ask the model to invent a more plausible midpoint without citations.
options.why: Incorrect. Fabricating a midpoint violates source and evidence discipline.

options.text: Cite both sources, explain the conflict, and require human judgment.
options.why: Correct. This keeps the evidence trail intact and makes the judgment explicit.

options.text: Drop both sources and rely on the base model's prior knowledge.
options.why: Incorrect. The base model is not a substitute for reviewed source evidence.

questions.q: A no-code agent is well written, but its knowledge base includes a broad mixed client drive that has not been classified as Green/permitted for AI use and has no exclusions. What is the core defect?
questions.source: No-code sharing controls and AI-use charter compatibility - approved Green/permitted sources, exclusions, only required tools, expected cases, and failure cases must be defined before sharing.

options.text: The role layer is always more important than the knowledge layer.
options.why: Incorrect. Role matters, but broad uncontrolled knowledge access is itself a serious defect.

options.text: No-code agents cannot use files under any circumstances.
options.why: Incorrect. They can use approved files or corpora when properly scoped.

options.text: The source perimeter, AI-use permission, and exclusions are undefined.
options.why: Correct. This violates source governance and AI-use charter compatibility before sharing.

options.text: The agent should be converted to image generation because files are visual assets.
options.why: Incorrect. The issue is access governance, not visual generation.

options.text: The only defect is that the agent has not been branded.
options.why: Incorrect. Branding is irrelevant to source and permission control.

questions.q: A coding agent's automated tests pass, but the change log shows edits outside the authorized files. What should the reviewer decide?
questions.source: Review gate and permission boundary - passing checks are only part of the evidence; the reviewer must also verify scope and unrelated changes, then accept, revise, pause, or reject the result.

options.text: Accept the result because passing automated tests prove that the extra edits are safe.
options.why: Incorrect. Tests do not authorize out-of-scope changes or prove that unrelated edits are acceptable.

options.text: Approve the authorized-file changes and ignore the other edits because they are outside the requested deliverable.
options.why: Incorrect. The reviewer must account for every change and cannot ignore a permission-boundary breach.

options.text: Ask the agent whether the extra edits were useful and accept them if it says yes.
options.why: Incorrect. Agent self-justification is not authorization or independent review evidence.

options.text: Pause the result, safely undo the agent's unrelated edits, narrow scope, rerun checks, and review again.
options.why: Correct. The scope breach must be contained and corrected even though the automated tests passed.

options.text: Keep the result but document the scope breach only after deployment.
options.why: Incorrect. Evidence review and resolution of out-of-scope changes must happen before acceptance or deployment.

questions.q: Why is image generation not an acceptable substitute for evidence in a consulting analysis?
questions.source: Agent tools - image_generation drafts or edits visuals when allowed; evidence workflows require sources, data, calculations, and review.

options.text: It creates visuals, not validated sources, calculations, assumptions, or claims.
options.why: Correct. Visual quality and evidentiary validity are different controls.

options.text: Because image generation is never allowed in any professional workflow.
options.why: Incorrect. The training allows visual generation or editing when appropriate and permitted.

options.text: Because image tools can access every source automatically, making citations redundant.
options.why: Incorrect. Image generation is not source retrieval or citation validation.

options.text: Because visuals do not need review if they are only internal.
options.why: Incorrect. AI-generated visuals still require review before use or sharing.

options.text: Because image generation is the same as code_interpreter, but less precise.
options.why: Incorrect. The tools serve different workflows.

questions.q: Why do monitoring and revocation matter more once an agent is shared?
questions.source: Shared deployment - sharing adds allowed users, monitoring, and revocation to the governed agent package.

options.text: They allow the agent to run without an owner.
options.why: Incorrect. Monitoring and revocation complement ownership; they do not replace it.

options.text: They make source review unnecessary after launch.
options.why: Incorrect. Source and output review remain necessary.

options.text: They are mainly cosmetic features for stakeholder reassurance.
options.why: Incorrect. They are operational controls for shared access and behavior.

options.text: They let all users modify the agent instructions freely.
options.why: Incorrect. Shared deployment should preserve controlled instructions and ownership.

options.text: They reveal drift and let the owner revoke access.
options.why: Correct. Monitoring and revocation are core shared-deployment safeguards.

questions.q: Which task should most likely stay in ordinary chat rather than become a no-code or coding-agent workflow?
questions.source: Switch rule - chat is enough for simple draft or review tasks; use agents or coding agents for recurrence, tools, files, commands, checks, scale, or evidence generation.

options.text: A recurring public-source target radar with fixed output checks and a named reviewer.
options.why: Incorrect. Recurrence and defined review make this a no-code agent candidate.

options.text: A short, low-risk rewrite requiring no files, tools, recurrence, or evidence log.
options.why: Correct. This fits ordinary chat rather than a heavier workflow.

options.text: A 70-company extraction requiring JSON outputs, logs, and sample QA.
options.why: Incorrect. Scale, files, and checks point to a coding-agent workflow.

options.text: A connector-enabled workflow that reads authorized CRM records and opens tasks.
options.why: Incorrect. Even when records are authorized, connectors and actions require agent governance.

options.text: A formula reconciliation across multiple workbooks.
options.why: Incorrect. Data/file processing and checks point to code_interpreter or coding-agent support.

questions.q: During a controlled 10-company coding-agent batch, sample QA finds that one profile has no source links and contains an unsupported value. What should happen next?
questions.source: Industrialized coding agents - retain logs and apply sample QA and stop rules during scaled runs; when evidence or schema checks fail, pause, investigate, correct the workflow, and obtain reviewer sign-off before resuming.

options.text: Pause, preserve evidence, correct the workflow, rerun sample QA, and obtain reviewer approval.
options.why: Correct. Missing provenance and an unsupported value trigger the stop rule; evidence must be preserved and the corrected workflow revalidated.

options.text: Let all remaining batches finish, because stopping would make the dataset inconsistent.
options.why: Incorrect. Continuing after a material QA failure can multiply the same defect across the remaining batch.

options.text: Correct that profile manually and continue without changing or retesting the workflow.
options.why: Incorrect. A manual patch hides the symptom but does not establish whether the workflow will repeat the defect.

options.text: Delete the failed profile and its log, then continue with the other companies.
options.why: Incorrect. Deleting evidence prevents root-cause review and does not resolve the underlying control failure.

options.text: Continue because one failed sample is acceptable whenever the batch size is ten.
options.why: Incorrect. No such automatic tolerance is defined; an unsupported value and missing sources require the agreed stop and review process.

questions.q: Which failure case is most important to test before sharing a source-backed no-code agent?
questions.source: Before sharing - expected and failure cases must be checked, with approved sources and exclusions, required tools only, and named owner/reviewer.

options.text: Whether the agent can generate a more attractive logo.
options.why: Incorrect. Visual branding is not the core failure mode for a source-backed workflow.

options.text: Whether the agent can answer faster if all tools are enabled.
options.why: Incorrect. Tool discipline is more important than unrestricted speed.

options.text: Off-scope requests, excluded sources, missing evidence, and unsupported claims.
options.why: Correct. These are the failures that protect source quality and governance.

options.text: Whether users can bypass the owner by rewording the prompt.
options.why: Incorrect. That would be a risk to prevent, not a successful test outcome.

options.text: Whether the agent can answer without source logs.
options.why: Incorrect. Source logs are part of reviewability, not a feature to remove.

questions.q: Which briefing style is weakest for a coding agent under the training's operating model and the AI-use charter requirement that documents be authorized before use?
questions.source: Coding-agent brief and AI-use charter compatibility - define objective, authorized files/folders in scope, allowed commands, output, evidence, stop conditions, and reviewer.

options.text: Inspect only this folder, update these two files, run these checks, and report changed files and test output.
options.why: Incorrect. This is a controlled and reviewable brief.

options.text: Make the project better; use whatever files and commands you need, then tell me when it looks good.
options.why: Correct. It lacks boundary, allowed actions, evidence, stop rules, and review criteria.

options.text: Generate a CSV using this schema from these authorized sources, stop on missing fields, and keep a log of exclusions.
options.why: Incorrect. This defines authorized sources, scope, schema, stop rules, and evidence.

options.text: Run a pilot on three records, compare to manual QA, and wait for approval before batching.
options.why: Incorrect. This follows the sample-check and review-gate discipline.

options.text: Do not access external sources; use only the provided Green/permitted files and list any ambiguous assumptions.
options.why: Incorrect. This explicitly constrains source access, document authorization, and ambiguity handling.

questions.q: A combined analysis uses public web pages and approved internal files, but its extraction table does not show which source supports each field. What is the correct review response?
questions.source: Evidence provenance and AI-use charter compatibility - the reviewer must be able to trace claims or fields to their source route, verify internal-file authorization, and see missing or conflicting evidence before approval.

options.text: Approve it because both the public pages and internal files came from permitted source categories.
options.why: Incorrect. Permitted sources do not make an untraceable extraction table reviewable or evidence-backed.

options.text: Add one general bibliography at the end without linking sources to individual fields.
options.why: Incorrect. A general bibliography does not show which evidence supports each extracted value or claim.

options.text: Infer the source of each value from the surrounding narrative and approve the table if the numbers look plausible.
options.why: Incorrect. Plausibility and reviewer inference are not a reliable evidence trail.

options.text: Withhold approval; require claim-level provenance, expose evidence gaps, and verify internal-file authorization.
options.why: Correct. The result becomes reviewable only when its evidence is traceable and the authorization of internal sources is confirmed.

options.text: Replace references to internal files with the model's prior knowledge so only public citations remain.
options.why: Incorrect. Model memory is not evidence and cannot replace the authorized internal sources used in the analysis.

questions.q: What is the most precise meaning of automation in the coding-agent conclusion?
questions.source: Conclusion - automation is repeated agent execution with logs, stop rules, owner, and human decision ownership; the agent can run the workflow, Advancy owns the decision.

options.text: Removing the human reviewer once a workflow has succeeded once.
options.why: Incorrect. Success once does not remove the review gate.

options.text: Letting the model decide when a workflow is mature enough to scale.
options.why: Incorrect. Scale decisions are made by humans using evidence.

options.text: Replacing logs with a final status message to save time.
options.why: Incorrect. Logs are part of automation control.

options.text: Running every task through a coding agent, even simple chat tasks.
options.why: Incorrect. The switch rule keeps simple draft or review tasks in chat.

options.text: Repeated execution with fixed controls, logs, ownership, and human approval.
options.why: Correct. This captures controlled automation rather than uncontrolled autonomy.
